name: ClickHouse Cloud API
what_it_does: ClickHouse Cloud management and query API for the high-performance columnar OLAP database, enabling analytics at massive scale over time-series, event, and log data.
use_cases: Running sub-second analytical queries over billions of rows of event data
Building real-time dashboards over log and telemetry data
Provisioning and scaling ClickHouse Cloud services programmatically
Ingesting high-throughput event streams for analytics
Analyzing product telemetry and user behavior at scale
not_for: OLTP transactional workloads (use Postgres or MySQL)
Small datasets where simpler tools suffice
Teams needing simple SQL without analytical query expertise
best_when: You need to run fast analytical queries over large volumes of immutable or append-only event/log data.
avoid_when: You need frequent row-level updates, complex transactions, or a general-purpose OLTP database.

auth.notes: Cloud Management API uses Bearer token (from portal). Query HTTP interface uses Basic Auth (username:password). Native protocol (port 9440) uses separate credentials.

pricing.notes: Usage-based pricing on compute and storage. Trial credits available. On-demand and reserved capacity options. Self-hosted open-source version available for free.

performance.rate_limits: No hard rate limits; scales with cluster size

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SQL errors are very descriptive with exact positions and suggestions. HTTP API errors include exception class, message, and error code. One of the better query databases for error quality.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: INSERT queries are idempotent if using ReplacingMergeTree or deduplication settings. Standard inserts append data.
known_agent_gotchas: ClickHouse doesn't support UPDATE/DELETE in the traditional sense — use ReplacingMergeTree or mutation operations
Eventual consistency in deduplication — duplicate rows may exist temporarily after insert
Query HTTP interface returns data in many formats (JSON, CSV, TabSeparated) — specify format explicitly
Large result sets should use streaming — avoid loading billions of rows into memory
Cloud Management API and database query API are separate with different auth mechanisms
Dictionary and Materialized View setup required for many optimization patterns — plan schema carefully
